Run Uniface Batch Applications on the iSeries

A batch application consists of at least an application shell (.aps file) that resides in a UAR, but normally there are also forms that are activated by the application shell in the same UAR.

You can make this UAR available on the iSeries using the methods described in Running Uniface Services on the iSeries.

To run a batch application:

  1. Ensure that licenses are available.
  2. Ensure that the Uniface installation library is in the library list.
  3. Ensure that all the UARs that contain your application shell and the forms it needs are in the [RESOURCES] section of your assignment file.
    The assignment file that will be used has the same name as your application shell, unless you use the '/asn=...'flag to specify a different one.
  4. Call the Uniface program using the following syntax:

    CALL

    PGM(UNIFxxxx/UNIFACE) PARM( UnifaceFlags StartUpShell ApplicationParameters )

    • UnifaceFlags—Uniface command line switches, as required. Each switch must be enclosed in single quotes. All Uniface flags must precede the application shell name.

      To run a pure batch application, use the '/bat' flag. It must be followed by another Uniface flag, such as '/pri=0'.

      If you do not use the '/bat' flag, it is technically not a batch application. In this case, a form is drawn internally, but is not displayed. Such applications will work as long as they do not require any user interaction. As soon as user interaction is required, the application is locked forever waiting for a ‘mutex’ and you will have to end the job with option 4 in WRKACTJOB and *IMMED for How to end.

      Therefore, if you do not use '/bat', you must take care in your ProcScript that your application never requires any user interaction.

    • StartUpShell—name of the application shell (without the .aps extension)
    • ApplicationParameters—application-specific parameters, as required. Each parameter must be enclosed in single quotes.
  5. Submit the CL command above using the ALWMLTTHD(*YES) option, or use SPAWN instead of CALL in the CL command in step 4.
    This is required because the Uniface program is multi-threaded.

An assignment file member with the same name as the application shell is read, as well as member USYS, unless the /asn flag is used.

For example, to run a batch application compiled into mybatch.aps, submit it to the job queue QBATCH as follows:

SBMJOB CMD(CALL PGM(UNIFLIB/UNIFACE) PARM('/bat /pri=63' MYBATCH 'arg1' 'arg2'))
 JOB(MYBATCH)
 CURLIB(UNIFLIB)
 ALWMLTTHD(*YES)

This example assumes that the application needs two arguments and you want $ioprint=63 output in your log file.

Related Links
  • /bat

    Start the application in batch mode.
